mnemonics: PIPE-LINE: Piping Internal/External Processes In a Logical, Integrated Network of Execution
keyPoints: Internal commands (like 'cd', 'echo') are built into the shell itself and execute faster without creating a new process.
External commands (like 'ls', 'grep') are separate programs stored in files, and the shell executes them by creating a new process.
Commands can be combined using operators like the pipe (|) to send the output of one command as input to another, enabling powerful, complex operations.
commonMistakes: Assuming all commands behave the same way, leading to confusion about why some commands (internal) can't be found in the filesystem and others (external) can.
Forgetting that piping (|) combines commands by connecting standard output to standard input, not by merging their arguments or functionality directly.
